author: @Thebigmanmetaldetecting
text: You can also make an invisible bone conduction headphone by using a tiny neodinium magnet and wraps of copper wire all you do is put the magnets in each ear right in ( they can be retrieved using the head of an iron nail) power the copper with a 9v battery and put the audio input through the loops of copper wire and with the wire around your neck  you hear the audio inside your head it's really freaky, it's along the same principals they use for high level security earpieces ( which is where I stole the idea from when I worked as a close protection body guard) it feels a little bit weird putting a tiny magnet in your ear at first but you get used to it

author: @ronrattink9327
text:
Hey Robert, I just happen to have a small useless drone with four of those tiny motors and thought I'd give it a try. I took a closer look at the how they were assembled and saw they have a nice gearing system under the props and decided to have a go at a possible wind generator conversion instead. I know, this is about bone conducting speakers but after removing the electronics and re-soldering the motor wires together to a red LED; I actually managed to get it to light up. woohoo.. 
4 baby tiny motors generated 3 volts or there abouts in a strong wind.. Nothing to shout about but still fun to try. I wonder what a proper sized drone with rewired motors for higher amps could do???
Anyway, thought I'd share .

That is a new one on me, but I remember using just the coils at school as an experiment, I can't remember how well it worked, but we did hear something.  Our physics teacher had some strange ideas when teaching, strangely, it got our attention.  He had plants doing all sorts of small voltage things, even lighting their own diode lights at night, charging a small battery during the day.
He had plants playing musical notes, which would change when you touched the plants or come near them, I never found out how he did that.

I stocked up on diy electronic parts, I may have something I can test this with. I'll check it out today. Thanks I love this and always wanted to try it.

replies.author: @NorthernKitty
replies.text: In fact, if you don't mind waiting FOREVER for shipping, AliExpress has 0615 coreless motors for about $0.80 USD each or a set of 4 for $2.92, plus a few dollars shipping.  Amazon has a set of 4 for $9.99, which isn't much more and you'd get them MUCH quicker.  However... I think Rob's suggestion of a cheap toy drone is a better idea.  As a bonus, you'll get at least one 200mAh (roughly) lithium ion battery (usually 3), a USB charging cable, a wireless receiver and transceiver, a control board for the motors... all of which you can play around with for use in other projects.  Don't think of it as "destroying"... think of it as "transformative re-purposing". 😄

replies.author: @FusionDeveloper

author: @stevetobias4890
text: I wonder if this works to help deaf people hear? It would depend upon the cause of deafness of course.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: yeah I suppose so

replies.author: @sofa-lofa4241
replies.text:
Many years ago deaf people were taught to 'hear' by picking up tiny vibrations with their lips,
The teacher and student would stand close together and both press their lips against a balloon, the teacher would make letter and vowel sounds at first and then later on fully formed words,
My uncle is profoundly deaf, he is too young to have been taught using this method but he knew someone at the deaf club who was

replies.author: @jamesbrown99991
replies.text: Jov Ven Vibrations from the bone-conduction phone bypass the eardrum. Some deaf people have bone-anchored (i.e. an anchor screwed into the skull) bone-conduction earphones, so they get plenty of bone music

replies.author: @stevetobias4890
replies.text:
Jov Ven with some forms of hearing impairment, it is the "hairs" in the inner ear that pic up the vibrations and then nerves carry these vibrations to the brain which then stitches it together into the audible waves that were transmitted.
With bone conducting the vibrations amplify to the fluid in the inner ear which is p picked up by those very same nerves so this does have applications. Just look at cochlear implants, it's similar in a way.
This could help old people losing their hearing due to the degregation of those small hairs that amplify sound to the inner ear fluid sacks.

replies.author: @sarchlalaith8836
@werxeh8027
replies.text: @sarchlalaith8836 Attaching it to the helmet should provide wonderful resonance anyway, assuming you remove padding from where your ears fit in. Incidentally, where Robert tests it here isn't supposed to be the best location for a conductive bone speaker, best place seems to be the top of the jaw bone just in front of the opening of your ear. Could work really well inside a motorcycle helmet with the padding keeping it pressed against the correct part. It is worth noting the motor can get a bit warm if it's surrounded by insulation though.

replies.author: @NorthernKitty
replies.text: Awesome idea!!  For those similarly interested, there are some nice flexible filaments out there that should be comfortable enough to wear behind the ear.  I'm guessing TPU would have the best balance of stiffness to hold the motor and flexibility to be comfortable, probably something with a shore hardness around 90A?  Anyone who hasn't printed with flexible filaments before, use slower print speeds, try to feed filament in as direct a path as possible (direct drive extruders preferred) and lower the retraction rates (sometimes all the way to zero) as this filament jams easily.
